title: Statistical Process Control Graphs
description: Statistical process control graphs, also called control charts, plot a factory process metric over time with a center line and control limits so readers can tell routine variation from unusual signals.
openingSummary: Statistical process control graphs (control charts) help factory readers watch a process metric over time and separate everyday noise from unusual drift.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A statistical process control graph, often shortened to SPC graph or control chart, is a time-ordered plot of a process metric. The chart shows a center line for the typical level of the metric and control limits that mark the band of routine variation. Points that stay inside the limits usually reflect common-cause variation. Points that break the limits, or form clear non-random patterns, are treated as special-cause signals that deserve a closer look.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Long-running factory work produces metrics that move every interval: completions, failures, queue depth, and token spend. Without control limits, every bump can look like an incident and every quiet stretch can look healthy. Control charts give operators a shared way to see whether a change is still inside normal process noise or whether the process has shifted enough to investigate.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: Imagine counting successful goal completions in each ten-minute interval while a factory run is active. Most intervals land near the same center line and stay between the upper and lower control limits. One interval then spikes above the upper limit after a bad retry storm. The chart makes that special-cause excursion visible against the earlier in-control stretch, so the reader can tell unusual behavior from ordinary jitter.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: SPC graphs show up wherever factory operators watch process health over time rather than as a one-off snapshot. Throughput, failure rate, queue depth, and spend series are common candidates when a run lasts long enough for a center line and limits to mean something.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: A control chart is not a one-off dashboard snapshot with no limits, and it is not a benchmark or eval leaderboard. It is also not a rule that every spike is a deploy bug. The chart asks whether a point is unusual relative to the process limits before anyone treats it as a special cause.
